1: Some time later, the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt offended their master, the king of Egypt — two royal officials fall into disgrace and are imprisoned — both in the prison where Joseph is held. The cupbearer (who tastes and presents the king’s wine) and the baker (who prepares the king’s food) are figures of honor who have fallen into dishonor through offense against the king. The fall from royal favor to imprisonment is the human condition in the presence of the Holy King. Isaiah 6:5: “Woe to me! I am ruined! For I am a man of unclean lips… and my eyes have seen the King, the LORD Almighty.” Those who offend the King are subject to his judgment; those who find a mediator between themselves and the King find restoration. Joseph will be that mediator in the prison.
2: Pharaoh was angry with his two officials, the chief cupbearer and the chief baker — Pharaoh’s anger at his officials places them under the threat of death (as v19 will confirm for the baker). The anger of the king that threatens death is the type of the divine holiness that threatens judgment: “our God is a consuming fire” (Hebrews 12:29). The two officials subject to the king’s anger need an intercessor who can mediate between them and the king’s wrath. Joseph will interpret dreams and thereby mediate knowledge of the king’s intentions. Christ is the ultimate intercessor: “he always lives to intercede for them” (Hebrews 7:25). The two officials who need someone to stand between them and the king’s anger are the type of all who need Christ’s mediation before the divine throne.
3: And put them in custody in the house of the captain of the guard, in the same prison where Joseph was confined — the two offenders placed in the same prison as the innocent Joseph. The innocent one sharing the prison of those who have genuinely offended their master — “numbered with the transgressors” (Isaiah 53:12; Mark 15:28). Christ is “crucified between two criminals” (Luke 23:33) — the innocent one sharing the execution of those who were guilty. The placement of the innocent covenant-bearer in the prison of the guilty is the structural setup for the type: one criminal restored, one executed, the innocent one present with both and mediating the interpretation of both destinies.
4: The captain of the guard assigned them to Joseph, and he attended them. After they had been in custody for some time — Joseph is assigned to serve the two imprisoned royal officials. The innocent prisoner serving the guilty prisoners — the posture of the servant who gives his service to those who are under judgment. Christ serves from his position among the condemned: from the cross he addresses both criminals (Luke 23:39–43) and promises paradise to the one who trusts him. The ministry to the imprisoned is both Joseph’s calling in Egypt and explicitly Christ’s identification: “I was in prison and you came to me” (Matthew 25:36). The servant who attends to those under judgment is the posture of the one who came “not to condemn the world, but to save the world” (John 3:17).
5: Each of the two men — the cupbearer and the baker — had a dream the same night, and each dream had a meaning of its own — the simultaneous dreams — one for each man, each with its own distinct meaning. The divine communication through dreams is the OT’s regular channel of revelation (Genesis 28:12; Genesis 37:5–11; Numbers 12:6). The same night, the same divine source, two different dreams with two different meanings — the type of the same gospel encounter that produces two different responses: one to life, one to death. The same Christ preached is “to one an aroma of death leading to death, and to the other an aroma of life leading to life” (2 Corinthians 2:16). The simultaneous revelation that divides two hearers into two destinies is the eschatological pattern that the prison dreams begin to trace.
6: When Joseph came to them the next morning, he saw that they were dejected — Joseph notices the prisoners’ dejected state before they speak. The pastoral attentiveness that reads the sadness of others before they express it is the type of Christ’s compassion: “When Jesus saw her weeping, and the Jews who had come with her also weeping, he was deeply moved in spirit and troubled” (John 11:33). “When he saw the crowds, he had compassion on them” (Matthew 9:36). The one who notices the suffering of others while himself imprisoned and suffering is the type of Christ’s solidarity with the suffering: he “sympathize[s] with our weaknesses” (Hebrews 4:15) from within his own experience of weakness. Joseph’s pastoral seeing from within the prison is the type of Christ’s compassionate seeing from within the incarnation.
7: So he asked Pharaoh’s officials who were in custody with him in his master’s house, “Why do you look so sad today?” — the question Joseph asks is the question of the one who cares about the answer. “Why do you look so sad today?” is the diagnostic question of the pastor/healer. Christ asks similar diagnostic questions: “What do you want me to do for you?” (Matthew 20:32, to the blind men); “Do you want to get well?” (John 5:6, to the paralyzed man). The question is not information-gathering (Joseph can see they are sad) but the opening of the relationship that allows the person to voice the need. Christ’s questions invite self-disclosure as the first step toward help; Joseph’s question opens the conversation that will result in interpretation and salvation for one of the two.
8: “We both had dreams,” they answered, “but there is no one to interpret them.” Then Joseph said to them, “Do not interpretations belong to God? Tell me your dreams” — “Do not interpretations belong to God?” — Joseph’s immediate deflection of the gift to the divine source. The prophet who attributes his interpretive gift to God is the type of Christ who consistently attributes his authority and knowledge to the Father: “My teaching is not my own. It comes from the one who sent me” (John 7:16); “the Son can do nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing” (John 5:19). The human interpreter through whom God speaks does not claim the gift for himself. Joseph in prison, claiming no glory for the gift, is the type of the one who “did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage” (Philippians 2:6).
9: So the chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream. He said to him, “In my dream I saw a vine in front of me” — the vine — the symbol of Israel in the OT (Psalms 80:8: “You transplanted a vine from Egypt”; Isaiah 5:1–7: the Song of the Vineyard). Christ identifies himself as “the true vine” (John 15:1–5): “I am the true vine, and my Father is the gardener.” The vine in the cupbearer’s dream produces grapes that become wine for the king’s cup — the type of Christ who is himself the vine that produces the fruit of the Spirit, the wine of the new covenant: “I tell you, I will not drink from this fruit of the vine from now on until that day when I drink it new with you in my Father’s kingdom” (Matthew 26:29). The vine in the dream points to the one who is the vine.
10: “And on the vine were three branches. As soon as it budded, it blossomed, and its clusters ripened into grapes” — the three branches, the budding, blossoming, and ripening. The fruitfulness of the vine — rapid, complete, moving from bud to ripe grape — is the type of the eschatological fruitfulness that comes through Christ the true vine: “I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit” (John 15:5). The rapid, complete movement from bud to ripe grape is the type of the gospel’s power to produce fruit quickly in those who receive it: “the word of God… is bearing fruit and growing throughout the whole world” (Colossians 1:6).
11: “Pharaoh’s cup was in my hand, and I took the grapes, squeezed them into Pharaoh’s cup and put the cup in his hand” — the cupbearer’s role: to put the cup in the king’s hand. The cup presented to the king by the servant is the type of the cup that is central to the new covenant: “This cup is the new covenant in my blood” (Luke 22:20; 1 Corinthians 11:25). Christ himself takes the cup: “Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?” (John 18:11). The servant who presents the cup to the king — the cup of blessing and favor — is the type of Christ who presents to the Father the cup of his own blood as the new covenant’s seal. The cupbearer’s dream contains the symbol of the Last Supper and the cross.
12: “This is what it means,” Joseph said to him. “The three branches are three days” — Joseph’s interpretation: the three branches = three days. The three-day interpretive key recurs throughout the Joseph narrative: three days here, three days for the brothers in custody (Genesis 42:17), three days before the good news. Three days is the consistent unit of the decisive divine intervention in the OT and NT: Jonah three days in the fish (Jonah 1:17; Matthew 12:40); three days of darkness in Egypt (Exodus 10:22–23); and above all, Christ’s three days in the tomb: “After three days I will rise again” (Matthew 27:63; Mark 8:31). The three-branch/three-day equation that Joseph offers is the OT’s clearest numerical type of the resurrection interval.
13: “Within three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your position, and you will put Pharaoh’s cup in his hand, just as you used to do when you were his cupbearer” — the interpretation: “within three days… restore you to your position.” The restoration to the original position after three days is the resurrection type. The lifted head, the restored position, the return to the original function — all of these describe Christ’s resurrection: exalted to the “name that is above every name” (Philippians 2:9), restored to the glory he had “before the world began” (John 17:5). The cupbearer who was under judgment and is restored to full function after three days is one of the clearest OT types of the resurrection’s structure: descent into death, three days, restoration to the original position of glory.
14: “But when all goes well with you, remember me and show me kindness; mention me to Pharaoh and get me out of this prison” — Joseph’s request: “remember me.” The imprisoned innocent one’s plea to the one being restored. The thief on the cross: “Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom” (Luke 23:42) — the request to be remembered by the one being elevated. The parallel is exact: an innocent one in prison asks the one being lifted up to “mention me” to the king. Joseph asks the cupbearer to mention him to Pharaoh; the thief asks Jesus to remember him when he enters his kingdom. The cupbearer forgets (v23); Jesus does not: “Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise” (Luke 23:43). Christ never forgets those who ask to be remembered.
15: “I was forcibly carried off from the land of the Hebrews, and even here I have done nothing to deserve being put in a dungeon” — Joseph’s declaration of innocence: “I have done nothing to deserve being put in a dungeon.” The righteous sufferer’s declaration of innocence from the prison is the type of Christ’s innocence throughout his trial. Pilate’s triple declaration: “I find no basis for a charge against this man” (Luke 23:4, 14, 22); the criminal on the cross: “This man has done nothing wrong” (Luke 23:41); the centurion: “Surely this man was the Son of God” (Mark 15:39). The testimony to the innocent sufferer’s innocence from every direction — from the judge, the criminal, the soldier — mirrors Joseph’s own declaration of innocence. Both are unjustly imprisoned; both are declared innocent by their own testimony and by others.
16: When the chief baker saw that Joseph had given a favorable interpretation, he said to Joseph, “I too had a dream: On my head were three baskets of bread” — the baker’s dream, prompted by the cupbearer’s favorable interpretation. The baker’s confidence in seeking interpretation after hearing the favorable verdict given to the cupbearer reflects the human tendency to expect favorable news. The two criminals at the cross both initially mock Jesus (Matthew 27:44); one changes and seeks mercy; the other continues in hostility. The baker’s expectation of a favorable verdict — based on the cupbearer’s experience — is the type of the presumptuous faith that expects grace without repentance. The same interpreter who gives life to one pronounces death on the other; the difference is not in the interpreter but in the one being interpreted.
17: “In the top basket were all kinds of baked goods for Pharaoh, but the birds were eating them out of the basket on my head” — the birds eating the bread intended for the king. The despoiling of what should have been presented to the king is the sign of the baker’s failed stewardship. The one entrusted to bring food to the king whose offering is spoiled before it arrives — the type of the worship that never reaches God because it is intercepted by the enemy. Christ warns about this: in the parable of the sower, “when anyone hears the message about the kingdom and does not understand it, the evil one comes and snatches away what was sown” (Matthew 13:19). The birds in the dream — eating the bread meant for the king — are the type of the enemy who snatches the word before it reaches its destination.
18: “This is what it means,” Joseph said. “The three baskets are three days” — the same three-day interpretation, but with a different outcome. The same three days that bring restoration for the cupbearer bring death for the baker. The identical time-unit produces two opposite outcomes: resurrection-type for one, death-sentence for the other. “The hour is coming when all who are in their graves will hear his voice and come out — those who have done what is good will rise to live, and those who have done what is evil will rise to be condemned” (John 5:28–29). The same event — the resurrection — produces two outcomes based not on the power of the event but on the condition of the recipient. The three days of the cupbearer’s dream and the three days of the baker’s dream are identical; what differs is what the three days mean for each.
19: “Within three days Pharaoh will lift off your head and impale your body on a pole. And the birds will eat away your flesh” — the unfavorable interpretation: “lift off your head” (not “lift up your head” as in v13). The same phrase used positively for the cupbearer (“lift up your head” = restore to honor) used negatively for the baker (“lift off your head” = execution). The cross has two aspects in the same way: “When I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself” (John 12:32) — the lifting up of Christ on the cross is simultaneously his exaltation (resurrection/ascension) and his death. One criminal drawn to him in faith; one rejecting. The fate of the baker — impaled on a pole, devoured by birds — echoes the curses of Deuteronomy 28:26: the covenant-breaker’s body left for the birds. The two criminals crucified with Christ face the same fate: one redeemed, one in the covenant-breaker’s place.
20: Now the third day was Pharaoh’s birthday, and he gave a feast for all his officials. He lifted up the heads of the chief cupbearer and the chief baker — on the third day, the birthday feast, the lifting of heads. The feast on the third day where judgment is pronounced is the eschatological banquet. Pharaoh’s birthday feast on the third day — the day of life-celebration — is also the day of the two officials’ judgment: one restored to life at the celebration-feast, one executed. The final judgment has the same structure: the great feast of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9: “Blessed are those who are invited to the wedding supper of the Lamb”) is simultaneous with the destruction of the beast (Revelation 19:20). The feast of joy and the judgment of condemnation occur together on the “third day” of God’s redemptive calendar.
21: He restored the chief cupbearer to his position, so that he once again put the cup into Pharaoh’s hand — the cupbearer restored: he puts the cup in Pharaoh’s hand again, exactly as before. The restoration to the original function — not to a different role but to the precise original position — is the resurrection type. Christ’s resurrection is not a transition to a different mode of existence but a restoration to glorious life: “This same Jesus, who has been taken from you into heaven, will come back in the same way you have seen him go” (Acts 1:11). The same body, the same identity, the same function — but now glorified. The cupbearer restored to his cup is the type of the resurrection body: the same person doing the same work, now in the context of the king’s restored favor.
22: But he impaled the chief baker, just as Joseph had said would happen — the baker’s execution, fulfilling Joseph’s interpretation exactly. The exact fulfillment of the prophetic word is one of the NT’s primary evidences for the Messianic identity of Jesus: “This took place to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet” (Matthew 1:22; Genesis 2:15, 17, 23; Genesis 4:14, etc. — the formula recurs throughout Matthew). Joseph’s two-prophecy fulfillment on the third day — both interpretations confirmed exactly as predicted — establishes Joseph as a reliable prophet of divine will. The reliability of the divine word through the prophet is confirmed when both predictions come true: “If what a prophet proclaims in the name of the LORD does not take place or come true, that is a message the LORD has not spoken” (Deuteronomy 18:22). Joseph’s word comes true for both; Christ’s word comes true for all.
23: The chief cupbearer, however, did not remember Joseph; he forgot him — the cupbearer’s forgetting — the chapter’s final word and the human story’s persistent tragedy. The one who was saved forgets the one who saved him. This is one of the OT’s most consistent complaints: Psalms 106:13: “But they soon forgot what he had done and did not wait for his plan to unfold.” “Was no one found to return and give praise to God except this foreigner?” (Luke 17:18). The ingratitude of the healed is the human constant. But the cupbearer’s forgetting is not the end: Pharaoh’s dream will force the memory two years later (Genesis 41:9–13). God’s purposes are not defeated by human forgetfulness — they simply wait for the moment when the forgotten one must be remembered. Christ is not defeated by being forgotten; he waits for the moment when every knee will bow and every tongue confess (Philippians 2:10–11).
